item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dreams are too random and there’s no evident connecting point between them. The creature seems to be talking gibberish too.

This seems pretty amateur-ish which surprised me considering the authors. I’m guessing I’m simply missing something important that would make my switch turn on.

But if that connecting point is this cryptic then it’s still bad writing.

On the plus side, recordings are nice, and the prose is better in some areas than anything I could produce, but that’s about it.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A team of scp-049… one in every plane, one physical, one mental, one emotional.

They all are the cure

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I enjoyed the logs, they didn't creep me out, even though it had pretty neat imagery. Maybe it's because my own dreams and recollections tend to be less coherent and more chaotic. It doesn't hurt the article, but it doesn't have much of an impact on me.

I did enjoy a lot more on my second re-read, when I knew the context of the title and image, which made a lot of the imagery more meaningful.

The third log confuses me, though. Is the man with the hat supposed to be the same as the entity? Because its behavior is very different to the rest of its appearances and kinda diminishes the tension and presence it builds.

Either way, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpicks
– hide block
  • "are to be isolated from uninfected individuals” #that’showthatworks
  • “After a while I see something laying in the road.” —> Technically, I think this should be “lying”. To lay is to set something in a resting position (transitive verb), and to lie is the act of resting in a flat position (intransitive; what’s happening here iirc). But this is an audio transcript and so can be passed over as a mistake of the one speaking.
  • While I think the voice acting itself is well-done, I think it still reads. That is to say, the delivery of the dreams is more like a piece of literature being performed, and not like a person recounting their dreams would say it. This is probably a result of the delivery being so polished.
  • I can’t figure out if the individual met in the 3rd addendum described in a hat is the same SCP-1 elsewhere described as having a hat, or if it is another character. I can’t imagine why it would be SCP-1, especially given that it seemingly describes it as something not itself, but it being a new character doesn’t answer anything for me either, just deepens the unknowns. May be my lack of close reading. Maybe, as others have said, this isn't supposed to have a deeper meaning, just a series of very nice and unsettling imagery.
Criticisms
– hide block
  • My biggest gripe is that the infectious part of this seems pasted on to the dream entity/world of this. Almost like it’s there as an excuse to act as a vehicle for the actual anomaly. It doesn’t help that the infectious component receives the primary designation while the entity that the whole article is about receives the sub-designation. For example, the discovery addenda are all about SCP-1 and do not technically mention how SCP is discovered. Again, in the description, 2/3rd of the paragraphs are devoted to the medical side of the infection, and only the last paragraph has to do with the remainder of the article. I get that it allows unsettling infectious quality of whatever this SCP-1 entity is doing to waking people, but that seems to be where its inclusion and purpose ends.
  • I hate that this part didn’t work for me, because I really appreciate the multi-sensory, multimedia inclusion of audio files in articles, but I read the page source of the article first, then went back and listened to the recordings, and, good voice acting aside, they took away from the creepiness. A good example would be the voice on the other end of the telephone that says “come upstairs”. That is freaky. But when I listen to the performance, all the unknowns provided by imagination from the prose alone are evaporated and I’m left with a comparatively pedestrian rendition of the previously-gripping moment (again, good voice acting aside).
Positives
– hide block
  • This is whole thing is really creepy to me. The scene where the arms of blackness are trying to grab or do something to SCP-1 but it is just ignoring them and staring at the narrator is really unsettling. The dream-logic in this is very believable (an odd thing to ascribe believability to, I know), and the imagery is powerful and made vivid by competent prose.
  • As said before, I think the voice-acting and the sound production of it (I think I hear at least some EQ on it) are well done. The individuals acting both have euphonious speaking voices, fit for the professionalism of public radio.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think those feelings are an essential, intentional part of the story. It's a dream, and is not meant to be understood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's candlejack with a hat and s

item.title: Pleased to meet you, won't you guess my name.
item.content:encoded:
And the comparisons to The Wizard of Oz are delicious.

+1 and will always love TheeSherm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there's anything to get, beyond "Eldritch thing does creepy dream stuff". The authors can feel free to correct me, but sometimes an article is about something that just… is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So on the plus side, this is extremely well written. The dream logs are vivid, disturbing, and all around more than enough for a +1.

With that said, I don't get it. It's creepy and we'll written enough to be great but I don't understand what's actually happening here. That could very well be my own ignorance, but I'm pretty lost. Mysteriousness in SCPs certainly isn't a bad thing, but I do feel as if there's a significant component missing here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Cimmerian and Kaktus for having me on board for this SCP! Ya’ll wrote great stuff, and I’m glad I was able to bring some of it to people’s ears.

You can see more of my work here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad Dr. Hostetler understands, because I certainly don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The imagery in the final dream gave me serious Junji Ito vibes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Double plus thanks to my Co-Author on this, djkaktus. We've been working on this on and off for a few months now and we we finally decided to pull the trigger. Thanks to all the folk who gave us feedback on the idea and draft.

I'm playing Dr. Isaiah Hostetler and TheeSherm plays Dr. Harald Lang. Special thanks to TheeSherm for providing us with the voice work for log 4.

The image is public domain and information for it is here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Satansowing.JPG
